title: Pre-merge security review pass over the regression-testing branch
description: Before opening the PR, the executor model audits the new attack surface introduced by this epic: production deploy automation, CF API token with deploy scope, GH Actions running on PRs (potentially from forks), new third-party deps and actions. No dependency on any specific assistant tool or skill; the executor uses its own security analysis capabilities.
design:
Security checklist (concrete items the executor must audit):

A. Fork PR secret exposure
   - Does regression.yml run on pull_request from forks? If yes, are CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID exposed to untrusted code in the PR diff?
   - Consider: skip secret-using jobs when github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name != github.repository, OR switch to pull_request_target with careful checkout of the base sha, OR mark the repo as fork-PR-restricted in branch settings.
   - Same audit for the deploy step specifically.

B. CF API token scoping
   - Verify the CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN is scoped to: Workers Scripts:Edit + Account Settings:Read, ON THIS ACCOUNT ONLY. Not a global token. Not Zone:Edit. Not DNS:Edit.
   - If overscoped, document remediation (create a new narrow token, rotate the old one).

C. GitHub Action supply chain
   - Are actions pinned to commit SHAs rather than mutable tags? (actions/checkout@v4 -> actions/checkout@<sha>)
   - List of actions in scope: actions/checkout, actions/setup-node, actions/upload-artifact, gitleaks/gitleaks-action, github/codeql-action/init, github/codeql-action/analyze
   - If using @v4 tags is acceptable for first-party actions/* (signed releases), document this exception explicitly.

D. New npm deps supply chain
   - @playwright/test: verify version is current and not yanked. Check it has no high-severity advisories.
   - Any transitive deps added by Playwright?

E. gitleaks coverage
   - Does gitleaks scan the PR diff specifically, or only the latest commit, or full history?
   - Is the action configured to fail (not warn) on findings?

F. Prod auto-promote risk
   - Can a malicious or accidental commit on main push to prod without any human gate?
   - Consider: require environment protection rule on a production environment so promote job needs manual approval; OR restrict who can push to main via branch protection + required reviews on .github/workflows/*; OR add a CODEOWNERS entry for .github/workflows/*.

G. Secret echo in logs
   - In regression.yml deploy step, does echo "$OUTPUT" risk printing any auth tokens that wrangler might include in stdout?
   - Verify wrangler does not print the API token in its stdout.

H. Branch protection / required checks
   - After merging this epic, which workflows should be required checks on main?
   - Document the recommendation in task notes (out of scope to configure GitHub UI settings, but recommendation in writing).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Each item in the design checklist has been considered and assessed.
2. Every identified issue has a disposition: FIXED (with commit ref) OR DISMISSED (with one-sentence reason justifying acceptance of the risk).
3. No critical or high-severity findings remain unaddressed.
4. All GitHub Action references either pin a commit SHA (preferred) OR are explicitly justified using a major-version tag in the dispositions.
5. Findings + dispositions saved to this task notes via bd update.
6. Any code changes keep T1-T9 acceptance criteria green.
notes:
Output format for task notes:

## Security findings

### A1: Fork PR secret exposure via pull_request trigger
- Severity: critical | high | medium | low
- Risk: ...
- Disposition: FIXED (commit abc1234, gated job with head_repo check) | DISMISSED (reason: repo is private + no external contributors)

### A2: ...

## Action pinning audit
| Action | Reference | Status |
| ... | ... | pinned to SHA | tag accepted (justification) |

## Recommended branch protection settings (out-of-scope to apply but documented)
- Required checks on main: regression/test, regression/promote, lint, security/audit, security/gitleaks, security/codeql
- ...

title: Pre-merge code review pass over the regression-testing branch
description: Before opening the PR for this epic, the executor model performs a focused code review of everything produced in T1-T9. Catches issues that automated tests cannot: subtle YAML mistakes, brittle stdout parsing, misleading assertions, race conditions. No dependency on any specific assistant tool or skill; the executor uses its own review capabilities.
design:
Review checklist (concrete items the executor must consider):

A. .github/workflows/regression.yml
   - Is the wrangler stdout grep for version_id and preview_url robust to changes in wrangler output format? Are the patterns matched against the actual format T1 captured?
   - Does outputs flow from test job correctly to promote job via needs:?
   - Is there a race between preview deploy returning a URL and that URL serving the new version (DNS/edge propagation)?
   - Does webServer in playwright.config.ts ever activate in CI when PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL is set?
   - Are action versions pinned consistently?

B. promote job (regression.yml)
   - Is the if: condition correct? Does success() refer to the entire workflow or just the needs: dependency?
   - What happens if version_id output is empty (e.g. wrangler stdout format changed silently)?

C. tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts
   - Does the tile-aborting page.route actually fire BEFORE Leaflet attempts to load tiles, or is there a race?
   - Does the addStyleTag inject before paint or after?
   - Could the masking be hiding real layout failures (false negatives)?

D. tests/e2e/smoke.spec.ts
   - Are the data-testid additions semantic? Minimal? Any speculative ones?
   - Does the data-derived count assertion actually fail if a day is dropped, or could it silently pass?
   - Are sleeps / waits using proper Playwright wait conditions, not arbitrary timeouts?

E. package.json
   - Is the deprecated deploy script still meaningful? Misleading?
   - Are the new scripts (test:e2e, test:e2e:update) discoverable?

F. .gitignore
   - Are all Playwright-generated dirs ignored? Any leakage of test-results/ into commits?

G. Cross-cutting
   - Any duplicated YAML between workflows that should be a composite action?
   - Any secret echoed to logs (e.g. CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN in OUTPUT=...)?
acceptance_criteria:
1. Each item in the design checklist below has been considered and assessed.
2. Every identified issue has a disposition: FIXED (with commit ref) OR DISMISSED (with one-sentence reason).
3. No critical or high-severity issues remain unaddressed.
4. Findings + dispositions are saved to this task notes via bd update roadtrip-5l1.10 --notes ... (replacing or appending these notes).
5. Any code changes made during the review pass keep T1-T9 acceptance criteria green (re-run the verify commands for any task whose files were touched).
notes:
Output format for the task notes update:

## Findings

### A1: wrangler stdout parsing
- Severity: medium
- Issue: ...
- Disposition: FIXED (commit abc1234) | DISMISSED (reason: ...)

### A2: ...

Files reviewed:
- .github/workflows/regression.yml
- .github/workflows/lint.yml
- .github/workflows/security.yml
- .github/dependabot.yml
- playwright.config.ts
- tests/e2e/smoke.spec.ts
- tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts
- package.json
- .gitignore
- README.md
- AGENTS.md
- src/components/*.tsx (only the testid additions)

Verify after pass:
- For each task T1-T9 whose files were modified during review, re-run that task's verify commands and confirm still green

title: Regression CI workflow: build, preview deploy, run Playwright, upload artifact
description: Core deliverable of the epic. Every pull_request and push to main exercises the test suite against a real Cloudflare Workers preview deployment. On failure, uploads the Playwright HTML report plus traces and visual diff PNGs as a downloadable artifact.
design: Single job: test. Outputs version_id and preview_url so T6's promote job can consume them. Parses both values from wrangler stdout using grep patterns confirmed in T1. Installs only chromium (--with-deps) to keep runner time down. Uses actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-node@v4 with node 22 (matches existing CF deploy requirement, see commit bdf7653). artifact upload runs only if: failure() with 14-day retention.
acceptance_criteria:
1. .github/workflows/regression.yml exists and is valid YAML.
2. Workflow triggers on pull_request and push to main.
3. On a test PR with no changes, the workflow runs to completion and exits green.
4. On a deliberately-introduced regression (e.g. a temporary test asserting wrong marker count), the workflow fails AND the playwright-report artifact is downloadable from the workflow run page and contains the failing test HTML report.
5. Workflow does NOT promote to prod (that is T6).
6. Repo secrets CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN (scoped Workers Scripts:Edit + Account Settings:Read) and CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID are configured and the workflow consumes them.
7. If T1 found preview URLs need auth bypass, the workflow sets the documented CF Access env vars on the Playwright step.
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: .github/workflows/regression.yml

Workflow skeleton:
name: regression
on:
  pull_request:
  push: { branches: [main] }
permissions: { contents: read, pull-requests: write }
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      version_id: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.version_id }}
      preview_url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.preview_url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '22', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - name: Deploy preview to Cloudflare
        id: deploy
        env:
          CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN }}
          CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID }}
        run: |
          OUTPUT=$(npx wrangler versions upload --message "PR #${{ github.event.number || github.sha }}")
          echo "$OUTPUT"
          # NOTE: adjust grep patterns based on T1 captured format
          VERSION_ID=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep -oE 'Version ID:\s*\S+' | awk '{print $NF}')
          PREVIEW_URL=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep -oE 'https://[a-z0-9-]+\.workers\.dev' | head -1)
          echo "version_id=$VERSION_ID" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "preview_url=$PREVIEW_URL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      - run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium
      - env: { PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL: "${{ steps.deploy.outputs.preview_url }}" }
        run: npx playwright test
      - if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: |
            playwright-report/
            test-results/
          retention-days: 14

Verify:
- Open a no-op PR, confirm green workflow
- Open a PR with intentional break, confirm red + artifact downloadable

title: Functional smoke specs: tab nav, marker render, day cards, slider behavior
description: Need at-least-this-much behavioral coverage so a regression that breaks tab navigation, marker rendering, the plan view, or the journey slider is caught before it ships. Pure functional assertions only; no screenshots in this task (visual snapshots are T4).
design:
Read trip data once at the top of the spec to derive expected counts and labels dynamically. Do not hardcode 9 days or specific city names. data-testid selectors preferred over text selectors for stability.

The 5 tests:
1. 'loads the homepage' - visit /, expect title to be 'Boston → The West → Boston', expect #root visible
2. 'switches between Map, Plan, Journey tabs' - click each tab, assert active indicator changes and corresponding view container is visible
3. 'Map tab renders all day markers' - switch to Map, wait for .leaflet-marker-icon, expect count to equal day count read from data file
4. 'Plan tab renders all day cards' - switch to Plan, expect [data-testid=day-card] count to equal day count
5. 'Journey slider advances and highlights the active day' - switch to Journey, press ArrowRight 3 times on slider input, assert [data-testid=journey-active-day] text matches day 4 label from data
acceptance_criteria:
1. tests/e2e/smoke.spec.ts contains exactly 5 tests, listed in the design section.
2. All 5 pass against npm run preview on localhost:4173.
3. All 5 pass against a deployed CF preview URL (set PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL and run).
4. data-testid attributes have been added ONLY to components and elements that the 5 tests assert on. No speculative testids.
5. Existing Vitest component tests still pass (npm run test:run exits 0).
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: tests/e2e/smoke.spec.ts
- MODIFY (only where needed for the 5 tests): src/components/App.tsx (tab testids: tab-map, tab-plan, tab-journey, view-map, view-plan, view-journey), src/components/DayCard.tsx (data-testid=day-card), src/components/JourneyView.tsx or JourneySlider.tsx (data-testid=journey-active-day, journey-slider)

Verify:
- npm run build && npm run preview &
- PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4173 npx playwright test smoke
- npm run test:run (Vitest still green)
- Audit: grep -rn data-testid src/ and confirm every result is referenced by smoke.spec.ts

title: Scaffold Playwright: deps, config, directory layout, scripts
description: All subsequent test tasks need the Playwright toolchain installed and a base config that targets two viewports (Chromium desktop and Chromium mobile) and reads its base URL from PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL. This task installs the toolchain and produces a config that parses successfully but contains no specs yet (specs land in T3 and T4).
design: Two projects in playwright.config.ts: chromium-desktop (1280x800) and chromium-mobile (devices['iPhone 14']). baseURL falls back to http://localhost:4173 for local runs. webServer block uses npm run preview with reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI so local runs auto-start the server but CI runs require an external URL. retries: 2 on CI to absorb tile-load flakes. expect.toHaveScreenshot.maxDiffPixelRatio: 0.01 as the default snapshot tolerance.
acceptance_criteria:
1. npx playwright --version prints a version.
2. npx playwright test --list exits 0 (no specs yet, but config parses without errors).
3. npm run test:e2e -- --reporter=list parses config without error.
4. .gitignore contains test-results/, playwright-report/, playwright/.cache/.
5. package.json devDependencies includes @playwright/test.
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: playwright.config.ts at repo root
- CREATE: tests/e2e/.gitkeep
- MODIFY: package.json (add @playwright/test devDep, add scripts test:e2e and test:e2e:update)
- MODIFY: .gitignore (add test-results/, playwright-report/, playwright/.cache/)

Commands:
- npm install -D @playwright/test
- mkdir -p tests/e2e && touch tests/e2e/.gitkeep

playwright.config.ts skeleton:
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';
export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './tests/e2e',
  retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0,
  reporter: [['html', { open: 'never' }], ['list']],
  use: { baseURL: process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL || 'http://localhost:4173', trace: 'retain-on-failure' },
  expect: { toHaveScreenshot: { maxDiffPixelRatio: 0.01 } },
  projects: [
    { name: 'chromium-desktop', use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'], viewport: { width: 1280, height: 800 } } },
    { name: 'chromium-mobile', use: { ...devices['iPhone 14'] } },
  ],
  webServer: { command: 'npm run preview', url: 'http://localhost:4173', reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI, timeout: 120_000 },
});

Verify:
- npx playwright test --list
- npm run test:e2e -- --reporter=list

title: Preflight: verify Cloudflare Workers preview URL is publicly fetchable from CI
description: The regression workflow depends on wrangler versions upload producing a URL that anonymous HTTP clients (GitHub Actions runners) can reach. If the account has Cloudflare Access enabled on preview URLs, Playwright will hit an auth page instead of the app and all tests will silently fail in a confusing way. This is the riskiest unknown in the epic. Verify before building the workflow on top of it.
design: Pure research / verification task. No code shipped. Output is the answer to: can CI runners reach preview URLs without auth? Findings persist in task notes so task 5 can implement against confirmed facts, not guesses.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A specific preview URL has been fetched anonymously and returned HTTP 200 with the application HTML (not a CF Access login page).
2. Task notes document one of: (a) preview URLs are public, no auth bypass needed; OR (b) auth bypass via Cloudflare Access service tokens is required, and the exact secret names plus header config are recorded for use in task 5.
3. The exact stdout format of wrangler versions upload is captured in notes (for the version_id and preview_url grep patterns task 5 will use).
notes:
Implementation steps:
1. wrangler login locally
2. npm run build
3. npx wrangler versions upload --message preflight-check
4. Copy the preview URL and the full wrangler stdout into task notes
5. From incognito browser and from curl without cookies: curl -I <preview-url>/ and curl <preview-url>/ | head -20
6. Expected: HTTP 200 and the index.html body (title contains Boston)
7. If gated: either disable CF Access for preview deployments on this account, OR document the service-token approach (env vars CF_Access_Client_Id and CF_Access_Client_Secret, plus extraHTTPHeaders block for Playwright config) in task notes for task 5 to wire up

Files: none modified. Output goes into bd update roadtrip-XXX --notes

title: Playwright regression testing with CI-driven preview deploys
description:
Set up automated regression testing for the deployed roadtrip app. On every PR and push to main, CI deploys a Cloudflare Workers preview via wrangler versions upload, runs Playwright smoke + visual regression tests against it, and (on main, only when green) promotes that exact version to prod. Replaces manual npm run deploy.

Also adds lint + security CI workflows on every PR, plus pre-merge code review and security review tasks over this epic's own code before the branch is pushed for merge.

Planning decisions:
- Runner: GitHub Actions hitting CF preview URLs
- Test mix: functional smoke + a few visual snapshots
- Target: CI deploys its own preview per push, tests it, promotes to prod only on main + green
- Triggers: pull_request + push to main
- Coverage: Chromium desktop (1280x800) + Chromium mobile (iPhone 14)
- CI code-review / security tools: free static analysis only (eslint, tsc, npm audit, gitleaks, CodeQL)
- Pre-merge gates (tasks 10, 11): executor model performs review using its own capabilities; no dependency on any specific assistant skill or tool

title: Documentation updates: README and AGENTS for testing, secrets, snapshots, CI
description: First teammate to see a red CI build needs to know what to do. First time someone intentionally changes the UI, they need to know how to update baselines. Without docs, regression test failed becomes ignore CI.
design: Docs reflect what was actually built across T1-T8. Goal is operational: a teammate unfamiliar with the project can, by reading README alone, run e2e tests locally and update snapshots without asking questions.
acceptance_criteria:
1. README.md has a Testing section covering: how to run unit tests (npm test), how to run e2e tests locally (npm run test:e2e), how to update visual snapshots (npm run test:e2e:update + commit PNGs).
2. README.md has a CI section listing all three workflows (regression, lint, security) with one-sentence purpose each.
3. README.md has a Required repo secrets subsection naming CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID with a link to how to obtain each from CF dashboard.
4. AGENTS.md mentions e2e tests exist under tests/e2e/ and must pass in CI before merge.
5. AGENTS.md documents the snapshot update workflow including: inspect new PNGs visually before committing.
6. AGENTS.md notes that prod deploy is automatic on green main push; one-off previews via npx wrangler versions upload.
7. AGENTS.md has a How to interpret a snapshot-diff failure subsection (download artifact, look at expected vs actual vs diff PNGs).
notes:
Files:
- MODIFY: README.md
- MODIFY: AGENTS.md

Verify:
- Hand the README to a fresh reader (or read it cold yourself) and try to follow it end-to-end without prior context
- Confirm every workflow added in T5-T8 is named in README CI section
- Confirm AGENTS.md mentions all 3 workflows by name

title: Security CI workflow: npm audit, gitleaks, CodeQL, plus Dependabot
description: Catch known-vulnerable deps, accidentally-committed secrets, and common JS security patterns before merge. Three independent jobs so each failure is diagnostic. Plus weekly Dependabot for proactive dep updates.
design: Three jobs run in parallel for fast feedback. CodeQL uses GitHub's hosted analysis (free for public repos, free credits for private). Permissions: contents:read, security-events:write so CodeQL can post findings to Security tab. gitleaks fetched with fetch-depth: 0 so it can scan full history. Dependabot kept lightweight: weekly cadence to avoid PR noise.
acceptance_criteria:
1. .github/workflows/security.yml exists and is valid YAML with three jobs: audit, gitleaks, codeql.
2. .github/dependabot.yml exists configuring weekly npm and github-actions updates.
3. All three jobs run on a test PR and exit green against current main.
4. A deliberately-committed fake AWS key (AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE) fails the gitleaks job.
5. npm audit --audit-level=high passes against current package-lock.json (if not, fix the vulnerable deps as part of this task).
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: .github/workflows/security.yml
- CREATE: .github/dependabot.yml

security.yml:
name: security
on:
  pull_request:
  push: { branches: [main] }
permissions: { contents: read, security-events: write }
jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '22', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm audit --audit-level=high
  gitleaks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }
      - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        env: { GITHUB_TOKEN: "${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}" }
  codeql:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
        with: { languages: javascript-typescript }
      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3

dependabot.yml:
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: npm
    directory: /
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
  - package-ecosystem: github-actions
    directory: /
    schedule: { interval: weekly }

Verify:
- npm audit --audit-level=high locally
- Test PR with const fake = 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE' triggers gitleaks fail

title: Lint CI workflow: eslint + tsc on PRs and main
description: Catch ESLint and TypeScript errors before they reach review or prod. Cheap, deterministic, free. Independent of the regression workflow so a lint failure does not block deploy testing and vice versa.
design: Single job, two sequential run steps. eslint --max-warnings 0 enforces zero warnings. tsc --noEmit is faster than full build since no output is written. node 22 to match other workflows.
acceptance_criteria:
1. .github/workflows/lint.yml exists and is valid YAML.
2. Triggers on pull_request and push to main.
3. Runs on a test PR with no changes and exits green against current main.
4. A deliberately-introduced ESLint warning (e.g. unused variable) fails the workflow.
5. A deliberately-introduced type error (e.g. const x: number = 'hello') fails the workflow.
6. Current main passes both eslint --max-warnings 0 and tsc --noEmit without modifications (if not, fix the existing issues as part of this task).
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: .github/workflows/lint.yml

Workflow:
name: lint
on:
  pull_request:
  push: { branches: [main] }
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '22', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx eslint . --max-warnings 0
      - run: npx tsc --noEmit

Verify:
- npx eslint . --max-warnings 0 passes locally on current main
- npx tsc --noEmit passes locally on current main
- Open test PR with no changes, observe green
- Open test PR with let unused = 1, observe red

title: Gated prod auto-promote on main + green tests
description: Once the test suite is trustworthy, prod should auto-promote only when (a) the push is to main and (b) tests passed against the version we would promote. Guarantees the version that hits prod is exactly the one CI just tested. Replaces manual npm run deploy as the path to prod.
design: promote job depends on test job (needs: test) and reads its version_id output. Gated by: if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && success(). Uses wrangler versions deploy with the @100% syntax for instant full rollout (no canary). --yes flag suppresses the interactive confirmation. Same CF secrets as T5.
acceptance_criteria:
1. .github/workflows/regression.yml contains a second job named promote.
2. Push to main with passing tests: prod (roadtrip.iamsayan.net) serves the new version within ~1 minute of the workflow completing.
3. Push to main with FAILING tests: prod is unchanged AND the promote job is skipped (not just failed) so no half-promote state.
4. Push to a non-main branch or PR: promote job does not run at all.
5. wrangler versions list shows the promoted version as active after a successful main push.
6. package.json deploy script is updated: either removed, or kept with an echo warning that CI is the canonical path.
notes:
Files:
- MODIFY: .github/workflows/regression.yml (add promote job)
- MODIFY: package.json (deprecate or remove deploy script)

promote job:
  promote:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && success()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '22', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Promote tested version to production
        env:
          CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN }}
          CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID }}
          VERSION_ID: ${{ needs.test.outputs.version_id }}
        run: npx wrangler versions deploy "$VERSION_ID@100%" --yes

Verify:
- Make a trivial no-op change on a branch, merge to main, observe prod URL update
- npx wrangler versions list (locally) confirms the new version is active
- Deliberately break a test on a branch, merge to main, confirm prod unchanged and promote job skipped

title: Visual regression specs with Leaflet tile masking and committed baselines
description: Functional tests catch broken behavior but not silent CSS or layout regressions. Visual snapshots cover layout. The Leaflet tile layer is async and uses third-party tile servers, so naive screenshots are flaky. Must be neutralized deterministically.
design:
Tile-neutralization strategy is the load-bearing decision. Two complementary mechanisms in a beforeEach hook:
1. page.route('**/*.{png,jpg,jpeg,webp}', ...) intercepts every image request; if the URL contains 'tile', abort it. Otherwise continue.
2. page.addStyleTag injects .leaflet-tile-pane { background: #ddeeff !important; } so the tile area renders a uniform color instead of empty white.

Wait for stable state before each screenshot: visit, navigate to tab, wait for tab-specific selector visible, page.waitForLoadState('networkidle'), THEN expect(page).toHaveScreenshot.

Baselines generated on a clean checkout using local npm run preview, then committed. Per-project subdirs auto-created by Playwright.
acceptance_criteria:
1. tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts contains 3 tests: Map tab matches snapshot, Plan tab matches snapshot, Journey tab matches snapshot.
2. 6 baseline PNGs are committed under tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts-snapshots/ (3 tests x 2 projects).
3. Inspecting one Map-tab baseline visually shows uniform blue-tinted background where tiles would be (no actual tile imagery), confirming the masking works.
4. npx playwright test visual passes twice in a row on a clean checkout with the committed baselines (no spurious diffs between back-to-back runs).
5. git status after the second run is clean (no new files generated).
notes:
Files:
- CREATE: tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts

title: Build JourneySlider component (prev + range + next + label)
description:
Slider + prev/next chevrons + current entry label. Pure presentational — owns no state, no map, no data. Can be built in parallel with JourneyMap and the parser work.

Spec sections: "Layout", "Components → New" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE: - src/components/JourneySlider.tsx (~40 lines) - Props: interface Props { count: number // total entries (>= 1) value: number // current index, 0..count-1 onChange: (v: number) => void label: string // e.g. "Day 10–12 · Black Hills" } - Render layout (matches the "Layout" diagram in the spec):
onChange(Number(e.target.value))} className="journey-slider-range" aria-label="Trip day" />
{label}
- Prev clamps: onClick = () => onChange(Math.max(0, value - 1)) - Next clamps: onClick = () => onChange(Math.min(count - 1, value + 1)) - src/components/JourneySlider.test.tsx - Use @testing-library/react + user-event (already in devDeps). - Test 1 (range fires onChange): render with count=5, value=2; user fires a change event on the range with value 4; expect onChange called with 4. - Test 2 (prev clamps): render with count=5, value=0; clicking prev does not call onChange (button is disabled) — assert button has 'disabled' attribute. Render with value=2; clicking prev calls onChange with 1. - Test 3 (next clamps): symmetric to prev — at value=count-1, next disabled; at value=2, next calls onChange with 3. - Test 4 (label renders): label prop is rendered as text in the day-label div. VERIFY BY: 1. npm run test:run — green. 2. npm run build — typecheck clean. DO NOT: - Add internal useState; JourneySlider is fully controlled. - Style anything in this task beyond the className markers; CSS rules come in the App-tab task. - Add keyboard handlers beyond what the native range input gives you for free. - Add a tooltip or popover showing the current day above the slider thumb. Out of scope.
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run test:run -- JourneySlider` — green. The new src/components/JourneySlider.test.tsx must cover ALL of these cases (each as a distinct `it(...)` block):
   - range input change fires onChange with the new numeric value
   - prev button is disabled at value === 0 and not called by clicks while disabled
   - prev button at value > 0 calls onChange with value - 1
   - next button is disabled at value === count - 1
   - next button below the cap calls onChange with value + 1
   - the `label` prop is rendered as text inside the day-label div
2. `npm run test:run` — green; full suite remains green.
3. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- src/components/JourneySlider.tsx is a pure controlled component with the documented props (count, value, onChange, label)
- No useState inside the component
- src/components/JourneySlider.test.tsx exists with the cases above
close_reason: Done: JourneySlider component and tests created. All 6 tests pass. Build typecheck clean.

title: Refactor: split parse-trip.ts into scripts/lib/ modules
description:
Mechanical split of scripts/parse-trip.ts (currently 254 lines, mixes hardcoded coords table, hardcoded route polyline, markdown parsers, and marker builder) into focused modules under scripts/lib/. No behavior change.

This is a prerequisite for the day-geometry work in a later task — that module needs to import COORDS from a cleanly-separated place, and we want parse-trip.ts to stay readable as it grows.

Spec section: "Parser refactor (prerequisite for the feature)" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE:
- scripts/lib/coords.ts
  - export COORDS (the Record<string, {coords, type}> table, ~60 entries — move verbatim from parse-trip.ts)
  - export ROUTE (the [number,number][] waypoint array — move verbatim)
  - export function matchesName(searchText: string, name: string): boolean (move verbatim)
- scripts/lib/parse-markdown.ts
  - export parseDayTable(block: string): Day[] (move verbatim)
  - export parseStageBlock(block: string): Stage (move verbatim)
  - export parseStages(md: string): Stage[] (move verbatim)
  - Import Day, Stage types from '../../src/types.js'
- scripts/lib/markers.ts
  - export buildMarkers(stages: Stage[]): MapMarker[] (move verbatim, uses matchesName + COORDS)
  - Import COORDS and matchesName from './coords.js', types from '../../src/types.js'

FILES TO MODIFY:
- scripts/parse-trip.ts — strip everything that moved. Final contents (~50 lines):
  - imports from './lib/parse-markdown.js', './lib/markers.js', './lib/coords.js' (for ROUTE)
  - the isEntryPoint guard block: read MD, call parseStages, call buildMarkers, load route-geometry.json with the ENOENT fallback (keep existing logic, including the routeSplitIndex computation), assemble TripData, write trip.json
- Existing tests that import from '../scripts/parse-trip' (if any) update their import paths to the new lib modules

VERIFY BY:
1. npm run parse — produces a trip.json byte-equivalent to the current committed version. (Easiest check: git diff src/data/trip.json should be empty after running parse.)
2. npm run test:run — all parser tests pass.
3. npm run build — typecheck clean, build succeeds.

DO NOT:
- Change any parsing logic, regex, or marker-building heuristic. Pure file moves and import rewrites.
- Add new tests in this task. Existing coverage is sufficient since behavior is unchanged.
- Touch src/ files. This is parser-only.

NOTE on .js import suffixes: the existing parse-trip.ts uses '../src/types.js' (with .js) because of the project's ESM/tsx config. Preserve that convention in the new lib modules.
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run parse` — runs without errors.
2. `git diff --exit-code src/data/trip.json` — exits 0 (byte-equivalent output). This is the PRIMARY correctness gate for this task: if the refactor preserves behavior, the regenerated JSON is identical to the committed one. If this fails, the refactor changed behavior — revert and re-do.
3. `npm run test:run` — green. All existing tests pass; no test files were modified (the only allowed change is updating import paths if existing tests imported from scripts/parse-trip directly).
4. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- scripts/parse-trip.ts is ~50 lines and contains only orchestration (read MD, call helpers, write JSON)
- scripts/lib/coords.ts exports COORDS, ROUTE, matchesName
- scripts/lib/parse-markdown.ts exports parseDayTable, parseStageBlock, parseStages
- scripts/lib/markers.ts exports buildMarkers

WHY THERE IS NO NEW TEST FILE FOR THIS TASK: the byte-equivalence check above is a full end-to-end test of the parser pipeline. It is strictly stronger than any unit test we could add. Do not add unit tests "to be safe" — they add maintenance burden without raising the bar.
close_reason: Done: Parser refactored into lib/coords.ts, lib/parse-markdown.ts, lib/markers.ts. Byte-equivalent output verified. All tests pass.

title: Build JourneyView component (composes Map + DayCard + Slider)
description:
The Journey tab's root. Owns the entryIndex state. Flattens stages into a list of entries, picks the current one, and renders JourneyMap + a small stage label + DayCard + JourneySlider.

Spec sections: "State", "Layout", "Components → New" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE: - src/components/JourneyView.tsx (~50 lines) - Imports: useState, useMemo from react; DayCard, JourneyMap, JourneySlider from siblings; types from '../types'. - Props: interface Props { stages: Stage[] markers: MapMarker[] route: [number, number][] routeSplitIndex: number } - Body: const entries = useMemo( () => stages.flatMap(stage => stage.days_list.map(day => ({ day, stage }))), [stages] ) const [entryIndex, setEntryIndex] = useState(0) const current = entries[entryIndex] ?? entries[0] const label = `Day ${current.day.day} · ${current.stage.name}` - Render:
Stage {current.stage.id} — {current.stage.name}
- src/components/JourneyView.test.tsx - Build a small in-memory fixture with 2 stages × 2 days each (4 entries), with realistic Day fields including mapCenter and mapZoom. - Mock JourneyMap to a simple component that renders its center/zoom as text (so we can assert on it without leaflet) — vi.mock('./JourneyMap', () => ...). - Test 1 (mounts at index 0): renders day 1's route text via DayCard; the mocked JourneyMap shows the day-1 center; stage label shows the first stage's name. - Test 2 (slider scrub): fire a change on the range input to value=2; the rendered DayCard now shows day-3's route text; the mocked JourneyMap shows the day-3 center. - Test 3 (stage boundary): scrub from an entry in stage 1 to an entry in stage 2; assert the stage label text changes. VERIFY BY: 1. npm run test:run — green. 2. npm run build — typecheck clean. DO NOT: - Persist entryIndex (no localStorage, no URL params). - Add navigation arrows on the map itself; that lives in JourneySlider. - Show markers grouped by current day, or any per-day filtering. Out of scope. - Reach into MapView or PlanView. JourneyView is independent. DEPENDS ON: JourneyMap task, JourneySlider task, day-geometry task (uses current.day.mapCenter/mapZoom).
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run test:run -- JourneyView` — green. The new src/components/JourneyView.test.tsx must cover ALL of these cases:
   - mounts at entry index 0: renders day 1's route text via DayCard; mocked JourneyMap shows the day-1 center; stage label shows the first stage's name
   - scrub: firing a change on the range input to value=2 (with a 4-entry fixture) re-renders the DayCard with day-3's route text and the mocked JourneyMap with day-3's center
   - stage boundary: scrubbing from an entry in stage 1 to an entry in stage 2 updates the stage label text
   - JourneyMap is mocked (vi.mock('./JourneyMap', ...)) to a simple component that renders its center/zoom props as text, so tests can assert without leaflet
2. `npm run test:run` — green; full suite remains green.
3. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- src/components/JourneyView.tsx with the documented props (stages, markers, route, routeSplitIndex)
- entryIndex useState lives in JourneyView; entries derived via stages.flatMap inside useMemo
- Composes JourneyMap + .journey-stage-label div + DayCard + JourneySlider in that order

title: Add scripts/lib/day-geometry.ts; extend Day type; regenerate trip.json
description:
Compute mapCenter and mapZoom per Day at build time, so the Journey tab can pan/zoom the map without any runtime coord lookup.

Today, 32 of 42 day entries have markers tagged with their day (we can derive a center from those). 10 entries have no day-tagged markers (Yellowstone, Grand Teton, Rocky Mountain, San Juans/Ouray, Sequoia, Yosemite, Redwood, eastern Oregon stage, Glacier buffer day, Boston return). We close that gap with a small day-string → location-name lookup that resolves coords from the existing COORDS table (no duplicate coord data).

Spec sections: "Per-entry coordinates", "Zoom heuristic", "Data flow" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE:
- scripts/lib/day-geometry.ts
  - Import COORDS from './coords.js', types from '../../src/types.js'
  - GAP_DAY_LOCATIONS: Record<string, string> = {
      '10–12': 'Yellowstone NP',
      '14–15': 'Grand Teton NP',
      '18–19': 'Rocky Mountain NP',
      '21–22': 'Ouray',
      '34–35': 'Sequoia NP',
      '37–39': 'Yosemite NP',
      '42–43': 'Redwood NP',
      '49':    'Bend',
      '55':    'Glacier NP',
      '57+':   'Boston',
    }
    NOTE: the dash in keys is the en-dash U+2013, matching how day strings appear in trip.json. Verify by inspecting the existing trip.json day fields before writing the literal.
  - export function pickZoom(day: Day): number {
      if (day.day.includes('–')) return 8
      const r = day.route.toLowerCase()
      if (r.includes('long') || r.includes('interstate')) return 5
      return 6
    }
  - export function computeDayGeometry(stages: Stage[], markers: MapMarker[]): void
    For each Day d across all stages:
      1. Find day-tagged markers: markers.filter(m => m.day === `Day ${d.day}`)
         If any, set d.mapCenter to their centroid [avgLat, avgLng] (just arithmetic mean).
      2. Else, look up GAP_DAY_LOCATIONS[d.day]. If present, set d.mapCenter to COORDS[name].coords. Throw if the name is not in COORDS (programming error — fail loud at parse time).
      3. Else, console.warn(`[day-geometry] no center for Day ${d.day}; using previous entry`) and set d.mapCenter to the previous Day's mapCenter (or COORDS['Boston'].coords if no previous).
      4. Set d.mapZoom = pickZoom(d).
    Mutates in place; returns void.
- scripts/lib/day-geometry.test.ts
  - Use vitest. Build small fixture stages + markers in the test body — do NOT import trip.json.
  - Test 1 (centroid): a Day with two markers tagged its day → mapCenter is the arithmetic mean of those markers' lat/lng.
  - Test 2 (gap lookup): a Day with day='10–12' and no markers → mapCenter equals COORDS['Yellowstone NP'].coords. Import COORDS in the test to assert.
  - Test 3 (fallback): a Day with day='zzz' (unknown), no markers, with a previous day having a known center → mapCenter equals the previous day's center; console.warn was called once.
  - Test 4 (pickZoom): day.day='5' route='Boston → Cleveland — long highway day' → 5; day.day='10–12' → 8; day.day='3' route='regional' → 6.

FILES TO MODIFY:
- src/types.ts — add to Day:
    mapCenter: [number, number]   // [lat, lng], filled at build time
    mapZoom: number               // 5 | 6 | 8, filled at build time
- scripts/parse-trip.ts — after parseStages and buildMarkers, before assembling tripData:
    import { computeDayGeometry } from './lib/day-geometry.js'
    computeDayGeometry(stages, markers)
- src/data/trip.json — regenerate by running `npm run parse`; commit the resulting file in this task.

VERIFY BY:
1. npm run test:run — day-geometry.test.ts passes; everything else stays green.
2. npm run parse — runs without "no center for Day X" warnings.
3. Inspect regenerated src/data/trip.json:
   - Every Day has mapCenter as a 2-element array of numbers and mapZoom as a number.
   - Spot check: stages[0].days_list[0] (Day 1, Boston → Cleveland) has mapCenter near Cleveland or a Cleveland-area marker centroid.
   - Spot check: the entry with day='10–12' has mapCenter = [44.4280, -110.5885] (Yellowstone NP).
   - Spot check: the entry with day='57+' has mapCenter = [42.3601, -71.0589] (Boston).
4. npm run build — typecheck clean.

DO NOT:
- Add a second coords table — gap lookup MUST resolve through COORDS.
- Filter/skip any day entry. Every entry gets a center, even if via fallback (warning logged).
- Make the zoom heuristic smarter than three branches.
- Modify any existing test fixtures or other parser logic.

DEPENDS ON: the parser-split refactor task (this task imports from scripts/lib/coords.ts).
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run test:run -- day-geometry` — green. The new scripts/lib/day-geometry.test.ts must cover ALL of these cases:
   - centroid path: a Day with two markers tagged its day → mapCenter is the arithmetic mean of those markers' lat/lng
   - gap-lookup path: a Day with day='10–12' and no markers → mapCenter equals COORDS['Yellowstone NP'].coords (import COORDS in the test to assert by reference, not by hardcoded numbers)
   - fallback path: a Day with an unknown day string and no markers, given a previous Day with a known center → mapCenter equals the previous Day's center; console.warn was called once with a message mentioning that day
   - pickZoom: day.day='5', route='Boston → Cleveland — long highway day' → 5; day.day='10–12' → 8; day.day='3', route='regional' → 6
2. `npm run parse` — runs cleanly with NO "no center for Day X" warnings emitted.
3. `npm run test:run` — green; full suite remains green.
4. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).
5. Inspect the regenerated src/data/trip.json (manual but precise):
   - Every Day has mapCenter (tuple of two numbers) and mapZoom (one of 5, 6, 8)
   - Day '10–12' has mapCenter [44.4280, -110.5885] (Yellowstone NP)
   - Day '14–15' has mapCenter [43.7904, -110.6818] (Grand Teton NP)
   - Day '57+' has mapCenter [42.3601, -71.0589] (Boston)

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- scripts/lib/day-geometry.ts implements computeDayGeometry, pickZoom, GAP_DAY_LOCATIONS
- src/types.ts Day interface includes mapCenter and mapZoom
- scripts/parse-trip.ts calls computeDayGeometry after buildMarkers
- src/data/trip.json is regenerated and committed

title: Build JourneyMap component (MapContainer + MapLayers + MapFlyTo)
description:
The map portion of the Journey tab. Renders the standard route + markers and "flies" to the current entry's center/zoom whenever its props change.

Spec sections: "Map behavior", "Components → New" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE:
- src/components/JourneyMap.tsx (~25 lines + small MapFlyTo helper)
  - Imports: useEffect from react; MapContainer, useMap from react-leaflet; 'leaflet/dist/leaflet.css'; MapLayers from './MapLayers'; MapMarker type from '../types'
  - Props:
    interface Props {
      markers: MapMarker[]
      route: [number, number][]
      routeSplitIndex: number
      center: [number, number]
      zoom: number
    }
  - Inner component MapFlyTo (defined in same file, not exported):
    function MapFlyTo({ center, zoom }: { center: [number, number]; zoom: number }) {
      const map = useMap()
      useEffect(() => {
        map.flyTo(center, zoom, { duration: 0.6 })
      }, [center[0], center[1], zoom, map])
      return null
    }
  - Default export JourneyMap renders:
    <MapContainer center={center} zoom={zoom} className="journey-map">
      <MapLayers markers={markers} route={route} routeSplitIndex={routeSplitIndex} />
      <MapFlyTo center={center} zoom={zoom} />
    </MapContainer>
  - Use className="journey-map" (styles come in the App-tab task).

- src/components/JourneyMap.test.tsx
  - Mock react-leaflet's useMap to return a spy object with a flyTo jest.fn() (vitest vi.fn()).
  - Mock MapContainer to just render its children (so the test runs without a real leaflet instance).
  - Test 1: rendering JourneyMap with center=[40, -100], zoom=6 calls flyTo with ([40, -100], 6, {duration: 0.6}).
  - Test 2: re-rendering with center=[41, -101], zoom=8 calls flyTo a second time with the new args.
  - Use markers=[], route=[], routeSplitIndex=0 for both tests (MapLayers handles empty arrays).

VERIFY BY:
1. npm run test:run — green, including JourneyMap.test.tsx.
2. npm run build — typecheck clean.

DO NOT:
- Render JourneyMap from anywhere yet — that wiring happens in the JourneyView task.
- Filter markers (no `filter` prop passed to MapLayers — Journey tab shows all markers).
- Add a moving "you are here" pin or progressive route reveal — both are explicit non-goals.
- Add animation other than the single flyTo with duration 0.6.

DEPENDS ON:
- MapLayers extraction task (imports MapLayers)
- day-geometry task (Day type has mapCenter/mapZoom — though JourneyMap takes them as primitives, the caller will read them off Day)
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run test:run -- JourneyMap` — green. The new src/components/JourneyMap.test.tsx must cover ALL of these cases:
   - rendering JourneyMap with center=[40, -100], zoom=6 calls the mocked map.flyTo with ([40, -100], 6, { duration: 0.6 }) exactly once
   - re-rendering with center=[41, -101], zoom=8 calls flyTo a second time with the new args
   - useMap is mocked via vi.mock('react-leaflet', ...) (or equivalent) so flyTo can be observed without a real leaflet map
2. `npm run test:run` — green; full suite remains green.
3. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- src/components/JourneyMap.tsx exports a default JourneyMap with the documented props (markers, route, routeSplitIndex, center, zoom)
- MapFlyTo helper lives inside JourneyMap.tsx (not exported) and uses useEffect with [center[0], center[1], zoom, map] deps
- The useEffect calls map.flyTo(center, zoom, { duration: 0.6 })

title: Refactor: extract MapLayers component out of MapView
description:
Pull the inlined <TileLayer>, the two route <Polyline>s, and the <CircleMarker> layer out of src/components/MapView.tsx into a reusable MapLayers component. JourneyMap will use the same layers in a later task; we extract first so the journey work doesn't copy-paste rendering code.

No behavior change for the Map tab.

Spec section: "Refactored" in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md
design:
FILES TO CREATE:
- src/components/MapLayers.tsx (~35 lines)
  - Props: { markers: MapMarker[]; route: [number, number][]; routeSplitIndex: number; filter?: (m: MapMarker) => boolean }
  - Renders:
    - <TileLayer> with the OSM url and attribution from MapView (copy verbatim)
    - When route.length > 1: two <Polyline> elements — outbound (route.slice(0, routeSplitIndex + 1)) with color #e05555 + dashArray '6 3' + weight 2.5 + opacity 0.8, and return (route.slice(routeSplitIndex)) with color #4a9eff and the same styling. Copy verbatim from MapView.
    - markers.filter(m => m.type !== 'city').filter(m => filter ? filter(m) : true).map(...) rendering each as a <CircleMarker> with radius 8, color/fillColor from MARKER_COLOR, fillOpacity 0.9, weight 1.5, with <Popup><MarkerPopup marker={marker} /></Popup>
  - Move MARKER_COLOR (the Record<MapMarker['type'], string>) into MapLayers.tsx and export it (so MapView can keep using it for legend chips if needed)

FILES TO MODIFY:
- src/components/MapView.tsx
  - Drop the inlined TileLayer/Polyline/CircleMarker JSX
  - Keep: filter state, filter pills, route legend chip, MapContainer wrapper with center/zoom/className
  - Inside <MapContainer>, render <MapLayers markers={markers} route={route} routeSplitIndex={routeSplitIndex} filter={m => filter === 'all' || m.type === filter} />
  - Remove the MARKER_COLOR constant (import from './MapLayers' if still used by the legend; otherwise just delete)
  - Target line count: ~65 (down from 98)
- src/components/MapView.test.tsx
  - If any assertion grabbed DOM that now lives inside MapLayers (e.g. polyline elements), the test still works because react-leaflet renders them into the same Map DOM tree. If specific assertions break, update them minimally — do not add new ones.

VERIFY BY:
1. npm run dev — Map tab visually identical: same colors (coral outbound, blue return, dashed), same markers, same filter pill behavior, same popups, same route legend chips. Compare side-by-side with main.
2. npm run test:run — green.
3. npm run build — typecheck clean.

DO NOT:
- Change marker filtering rules (the 'city' type is still always hidden — move that filter into MapLayers as documented above)
- Change colors, weights, dashArray, opacity, radius, or any visual parameter
- Add tests for MapLayers in this task. Existing MapView tests already exercise the rendering.
- Touch unrelated files.
acceptance_criteria:
DONE CHECKLIST — executor must run each command and confirm it passes before closing this task:

1. `npm run test:run` — green. The existing src/components/MapView.test.tsx is the safety net for this refactor: it renders MapView (which transitively renders MapLayers) and asserts on the rendered DOM. If MapView.test.tsx fails after the extraction, behavior changed — revert and reconsider. You MAY make minimal mechanical edits to MapView.test.tsx if assertions referenced elements that moved (e.g. a selector that no longer matches because of a wrapper change), but you may not loosen or delete assertions.
2. `npm run build` — green (typecheck clean).
3. Manual visual check via `npm run dev` on the Map tab — identical to main: route colors (coral outbound, blue return) with dashed style, marker colors per type, filter pill behavior, popups, route legend chips. Compare side-by-side with the pre-refactor view.

OUTPUT SHAPE (verifiable by inspection):
- src/components/MapLayers.tsx exists with the documented props (markers, route, routeSplitIndex, optional filter)
- src/components/MapView.tsx uses <MapLayers> instead of inlining; line count drops to ~65
- MARKER_COLOR is exported from MapLayers.tsx

WHY THERE IS NO NEW TEST FILE FOR THIS TASK: the existing MapView tests already exercise the rendering this refactor touches. Adding a parallel MapLayers test would duplicate coverage without raising the bar. A new test for MapLayers will land in the next task (JourneyMap.test.tsx) when it gains a second consumer.
close_reason: Done: MapLayers component extracted. All MapView tests pass. Build typecheck clean.

title: Journey tab: scrub-through-trip slider
description:
Add a third tab — "Journey" — alongside Plan and Map. It lets the user step through the trip's 42 day-entries with a slider. As the slider moves, the map auto-pans/zooms to the entry's location and a day card below shows the entry.

Full design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-journey-tab-design.md

Scope:
- New components: JourneyView, JourneyMap (with internal MapFlyTo), JourneySlider
- Reused: DayCard (unchanged)
- App.tsx gains a third tab; types.ts gains mapCenter/mapZoom on Day
- src/index.css gains a small set of .journey-* rules

Prerequisites (folded into the work as their own tasks because they keep the codebase clean):
1. Split scripts/parse-trip.ts (254 lines, 4 concerns) into scripts/lib/{coords,parse-markdown,markers}.ts
2. Extract src/components/MapLayers.tsx out of MapView.tsx so JourneyMap can reuse it instead of copy-pasting tile/polyline/marker code

Non-goals (explicit):
- No auto-play, speed controls, or animation loop
- No filtering markers by day; no progressive route reveal; no moving "you are here" pin
- No cross-linking Plan/Map ↔ Journey
- No URL state or persistence

The executor must read the spec before starting. Each child task has its own acceptance criteria, files, and approach.
